title: 7 1 Laplace transforms
body:
TOPIC 7 : LAPLACE TRANSFORMS I 7.1 LAPLACE TRANSFORMS

LAPLACE TRANSFORMS

Z ∞ L{f (t)} = e−st f (t)dt = F (s)

f (t) F (s) 1 1/s t 1/s2 tm m!/sm+1 tν , (ν > −1) Γ(ν + 1)/sν+1 e−at 1/(s + a) sin bt b/(s2 + b2 ) cos bt s/(s2 + b2 ) sinh bt b/(s2 − b2 ) cosh bt s/(s2 − b2 ) sin bt − bt cos bt 2b3 /(s2 + b2 )2 sin bt + bt cos bt 2bs2 /(s2 + b2 )2 t sin bt 2bs/(s2 + b2 )2 te−at 1/(s + a)2 u(t − c) e−cs /s e−at f (t) F (s + a) tf (t) −F 0 (s) f (t − c)u(t − c) e−cs F (s) f 0 (t) sF (s) − f (0) f 00 (t) s2 F (s) − sf (0) − f 0 (0) f 000 (t) s3 F (s) − s2 f (0) − sf 0 (0) − f 00 (0) Z t f (τ )dτ F (s)/s

The Laplace transform is a very useful technique that allows us to transform a function f (t) into another form F (s). The two forms of the function are completely equivalent and we are free to move from one to the other as need be. As we shall see, the Laplace transform is particularly effective in problems involving discontinuous changes in the function f (t) and so is extremely useful in applications of electrical engineering where variables (voltage, current, etc) can switch on or off instantaneously.

The definition on the Laplace transform is Z∞ L{f (t)} = F (s) = e−st f (t) dt

That is we integrate the function f against a decaying exponential function all the way from 0 to ∞.

We denote the original function by f (t) and the transformed object F (s). To keep track of where we are, we use t as the original variable and the transformed variable is s. Just like differentiation and integration, it is a linear process so that

L {af (t) + bg(t)} = aL {f (t)} + bL {g(t)} .

In practice, we generally do not use the integral definition to calculate Laplace transforms. Instead we will use a table of Laplace transforms (and their inverses). This table is printed at the start of these lecture notes and in your tutorial problems. You will also have a copy of this table in the exam.

But first a little revision on integration by parts and improper integrals.

Example 1 Evaluate each of the following definite integrals: R1 a) 0 3te−7t dt. R∞ b) 0 3te−7t dt.

One thing to remember is that the exponential function goes to zero much faster than any polynomial goes to infinity!

3 24 3 F a) − e−7 b) F 49 49 49

Example 2 Find the Laplace transform of 4t, firstly via the definition and then by using the table of Laplace transforms.

F F s2

You can see from the above example that the Laplace transform is an improper integral (since we are integrating all the way to infinity). What saves us is that e−st converges to zero very aggressively and many of our standard functions (certainly all of the polynomials, sin and cos) are quickly defeated by e−st . In general the Laplace transform of f will exist provided that f does not grow too rapidly as t → ∞.

Observe also that the integral is with respect to t only, but the integrand involves both s and t. Thus, the Laplace transform will depend on s. Notice also that we assume that t ≥ 0. The variable t usually represents time so this quite natural. A nice thing about Laplace transforms (as opposed to derivatives for example) is that they do not get too worried when faced with discontinuities.

 7, 3 ≤ t ≤ 4; Example 3 Let f (t) = 0, otherwise. Sketch the function and find its Laplace transform directly from the integral definition.

7(e−3s − e−4s ) F F s

Example 4 Use the table to find the Laplace transform of

f (t) = 7t2 + cos(4t) − 5 sinh(3t) + 2.

14 s 15 2 F 3 + 2 − 2 + F s s + 16 s − 9 s

Example 5 Use the table to find the Laplace transform of √ f (t) = 3e7t − 6te−2t + t.

3 6 Γ( 32 ) F − + 3 F s − 7 (s + 2)2 s2

Note that the gamma function Γ is an extension of the concept of factorials beyond the R∞ positive integers. Its definition is Γ(ν + 1) = e−x xν dx and it is usually evaluated by computer or tables. If ν is a positive integer then Γ(ν + 1) = n!

One final note is that the concept of an arbitrary constant C plays no role whatsoever in the theory of Laplace transforms! All of the integrals are definite integrals, so there are no unknown constants of integration. You can now do Q 95 and 96 a,b,c and d
